text: much fun again. They’ll call her the Black Dahlia, but her real name’s Elizabeth. Betty, to friends. And soon, once the whole world knows about her, knows all the secrets she never told them, she’ll have to wonder if that makes them her friends too. Like it or not, Betty’s about to have a lot of new friends. They’ll print her picture, a glamour shot from when she was still alive. You know the one. A faraway look in her eyes, a smile on her lips. A real smile this time, not the one etched into her face, the one she can’t escape. “Why don’t you smile more, darling? You look so much prettier when you smile.” But even as they take away her body, Betty doesn’t go with them. She’s still back at the crime scene, an invisible face in the crowd, watching quietly as the reporters gather closer, their hot flashbulbs shattering into a thousand jagged pieces. Betty wants to stay, she wants to understand, but she doesn’t get to linger here. The fading afternoon air shimmers suddenly, and something pulls at her like fishhooks in her skin, dragging her off through the ether. That’s because there are other places where she’s expected now. # Her second haunted house is all stone and shadow, tucked away near the Santa Monica Mountains. They say the property was designed by the son of Frank Lloyd Wright, but Betty doesn’t know for sure, because she never gets to see the whole place. She’s exiled to the damp basement beneath a row of splintered rafters, everything strange and gray. An echo of footsteps overhead, and Betty crawls back in the dark, hiding from him. The man who murdered her. He’ll come back for her, he’ll do it all again. She never gets a good look at his face, no matter how many times she relives this moment. It’s so impersonal what he does to her. So intimate too. As she cowers beneath the stairs, the air stinking of black mold and melancholy, Betty thinks how it isn’t fair. There should be no walls, no roof, no doorway to hold her in. She’s a ghost. There should be some freedom in that. But she belongs to the world now, and the world loves a mystery. She’s become a yellowed corpse for yellow journalists. A headline that never gets old. Yet even after a hundred stories, there are things about her the papers never bother to mention. Little things, all those inconsequential details that define a life. They don’t ask about her favorite color. (It was blue, she thinks, though she has trouble remembering now. She has trouble remembering so much, as though the ghost she’s become is no more than a stranger to her.) Or her first kiss. (He was just a silly nothing boy, a nobody, a cipher, but then again, aren’t we all?) Or how she had asthma growing up, so the doctors recommended she abandon the brutal Massachusetts winters for the Florida sun. (The warmer weather didn’t help as much as she hoped. Nothing ever helped as much as she hoped.) Or how she liked to play make-believe as a little girl. A few people even claimed she wanted to be an actress someday, because why not? Los Angeles is a city of dreams, a city of angels. (Isn’t it?) Or maybe she didn’t want to be an actress at all. Maybe that’s just another rumor about her. One thing for sure: she got famous all right, just not in the way she ever wanted to. Betty’s not the only girl to be murdered in Hollywood— Oh, honey, those come a dime a dozen —but she’s the one the headlines hold onto. The cold case that’s never quite forgotten. The Black Dahlia’s remembered, she’s worth remembering. Settled back in the gloom of eternity, Betty wonders if being remembered is a blessing or a curse. # Her third haunted house is a hotel, a place she never remembers visiting. But they say she went there, and they say she might have died there too, and it turns out their theories—whoever they are—are the only things that matter. Their theories are what drag her from one place to another, wrench her through the darkness, a restless phantom at their beck and call. Before now, Betty was like the rest of us: she assumed ghosts were forever shackled to a single place. But she doesn’t have a solitary haunted house. She has a multitude of them, one for every theory they invent about her. That’s because nobody knows for sure where she died, not even her. Betty wishes she could remember what came before. She wishes she could remember the last time she was happy. On the good days, if she closes her eyes, she can almost hear it. The parade on D-Day. The whoops of joy, the acrid stench of car exhaust on the streets of Hollywood, a cavalcade of Chevrolets and Cadillacs going down the boulevard, streamers in the air. If you’ve done your research, you’ll know there’s even allegedly a grainy video of her from that afternoon. In the back of an automobile, she’s with a group of other girls, grinning and celebrating, all the promise of the universe brimming in her heart. (Betty hasn’t seen the video, and she doesn’t care to. These days, she doubts she would recognize her own face anymore.) # Sometimes, when the headlines wane for a moment and nobody’s looking, Betty can slip away for a little while. Into the endless night, she goes wandering, past the cracked storefront windows and along Hollywood Boulevard, her ethereal fingers tracing the stars on the Walk of Fame, touching the names of the matinee idols from her day. Clark Gable. Lauren Bacall. Veronica Lake. She pretends they’re still alive. She pretends she is too, a game of make-believe where she’s merely a girl, not a ghost, not a gruesome legend, not this thing they’ve turned her into.
